titel:
Compensation for radio and television companies due to private copying.

This refers to the compensation paid to radio and television companies when their content is copied privately by individuals. This is often a legal requirement to allow for private copying while ensuring that the original content creators are fairly compensated.
html:
The government proposes new regulations in the field of copyright, which would include radio and television companies in the compensation scheme for private copying. This would grant them the same right to compensation as other eligible copyright holders. The proposal aims to align Swedish law with a ruling from the European Court of Justice. The proposed amendments to the law are scheduled to come into effect on January 1, 2026.

The proposal also includes a clarification of when the government can issue regulations regarding the application of copyright laws with respect to other countries. This amendment is proposed to come into effect on July 1, 2025.

Proposed Resolutions for the Parliament

Prop. 2024/25:137

Government Proposal: The Riksdag (Swedish Parliament) shall approve the government's proposal for an amendment to the Act (1960:729) on Copyright in Literary and Artistic Works.

Prop. 2024/25:137 2 Proposed Act Amending the Act (1960:729) on Copyright in Literary and Artistic Works

Hereby, it is prescribed that sections 48, 61, and 62 of the Act (1960:729) on Copyright in Literary and Artistic Works shall have the following wording:

Current Wording Proposed Wording

48 § 1 A radio or television company has, subject to the limitations prescribed in this Act, an exclusive right to dispose of a sound radio or television broadcast by: A radio or television company has, subject to the limitations prescribed in this Act, an exclusive right to dispose of a sound radio or television broadcast by:

  1. recording the broadcast on a device that allows it to be replayed,
  2. producing copies of a recording of the broadcast,
  3. distributing copies of a recording of the broadcast to the public,
  4. allowing retransmission or a performance for the public in places open to the public for an admission fee, or
  5. allowing a recording of the broadcast to be transmitted over wired or wireless means to the public in such a way that individuals can access the recording from a location and at a time of their choosing.

The rights referred to in paragraphs 2, 3, and 5 of the first paragraph apply until the end of the fiftieth year after the year in which the broadcast took place.

The provisions of section 2, second paragraph, sections 6-9, section 11, second paragraph, 11a, 12, 13, and 15a-16, section 16a, third paragraph, sections 16e-17c, 17e, 21, 22, 25-26b, 26e, 26k-26m, 42a, 42b, 42d, and 42g-42k shall apply to sound radio and television broadcasts referred to in this section.

When a copy of a recording, according to this section, has been transferred within the European Economic Area with the company's consent, the copy may be further distributed. If a radio or television company has a claim for compensation for such a further distribution as referred to in section 42f and which has taken place with the company's consent, the company shall make its claim at the same time as the claims referred to in section 42a, third paragraph.

61 § 2 The provisions of sections 45, 47, and 48 are applicable to performances, sound recordings, and sound radio and television broadcasts that take place in:
tldr: The government wants to grant radio and television companies the right to receive payments when individuals make private copies of their broadcasts. This is being done to comply with regulations from the European Union.
lattlast:
The government has proposed changes to copyright laws. This means that companies broadcasting radio and television will be entitled to compensation when individuals make private copies of their programs. This is part of a system designed to ensure that copyright holders are paid when their works are copied privately.

The proposal aims to align Swedish law with a ruling from the European Court of Justice. The goal is to ensure that the rules are consistent across Europe. The government also wants to clarify when it has the authority to set rules regarding the application of the law in other countries.

If the proposal is approved, the new rules regarding the government's authority to issue instructions will come into effect on July 1, 2025. The new rules regarding compensation for radio and television companies are planned to take effect on January 1, 2026.
